schools.summary: The gracious laws given after the rebellion of the spies — 'the LORD spake unto Moses' coming immediately after the murmuring over the spies' report (Num 14:1), the oath that the rebels should fall in the wilderness, and the defeat at Hormah, God giving laws for the worship of the land in the very shadow of judgment (Gill, Keil); the law beginning encouragingly 'when ye come into the land,' a word full of grace, assuring their children they would surely enter though the present generation die (Henry); the chapter partly repeating and partly gathering into one place precepts of sacrifice already given in Leviticus (Calvin); God not casting off his people utterly for their rebellion but renewing the covenant promise by these very laws, mercy rejoicing over judgment.

themes: the land still to be given

schools.summary: The promise of the land renewed — 'when ye be come into the land of your habitations, which I give unto you'; 'I give' meaning 'I will certainly give,' notwithstanding the great provocation of the spies' rebellion, the laws of sacrifice repeated as a pledge that through the appointed offerings God would be reconciled and bring them in (Wesley); addressed chiefly to the younger sort, those under twenty, who alone would live to enter, the older sworn to die in the wilderness (Num 14:29, Gill); several offerings here prescribed not meant for the wilderness, lacking settled agriculture, but for the promised land (Clarke); God in the act of judging the rebels comforting the faithful remnant with the renewed promise, the gift sure, the covenant unbroken.

themes: when ye be come into the land
the promise renewed to the young

schools.summary: The occasions of the offerings — 'a burnt offering, or a sacrifice in performing a vow... or in your solemn feasts'; the burnt offering wholly consumed upon the altar, distinguished from the other offerings (peace or thank offerings) of which part was eaten, 'a sacrifice' here the peace offering (Gill, Wesley); the occasions — in fulfilment of a vow, as a free and voluntary gift, or at the appointed festivals, the law covering the whole range of the people's sacrifices, occasional and stated, vowed and free; the end — 'to make a sweet savour unto the LORD,' that the offering be acceptable; the fuller laws referred to Leviticus (Lev 1:2, Clarke); the typology — the sweet savour pointing to Christ, whose offering was a sweet-smelling savour (Eph 5:2).

themes: a freewill offering, or in your feasts
a sweet savour unto the LORD

schools.summary: The meat offering with a lamb — 'a meat offering of a tenth deal of flour mingled with the fourth part of an hin of oil'; with a lamb, the meat offering a tenth of an ephah of fine flour (about five pints, Wesley) mixed with a quarter-hin of oil, and a like measure of wine for a drink offering; the animal sacrifice not offered alone but with its accompanying meat and drink offerings of flour, oil, and wine, the fruits of the field joined to the beast, the whole produce of the land brought to God together; these ordered for the lamb offered in a vow, freewill offering, or feast (Ellicott); the meat offering owning God as the giver of bread, the drink offering as the giver of gladness; the typology — flour, oil, and wine pointing to Christ the bread and true vine, and the gladness of the Spirit.

themes: a meat offering of a tenth deal of flour
the fruits of the field with the sacrifice

schools.summary: The drink offering with a lamb — 'the fourth part of an hin of wine for a drink offering... for one lamb' (with a lamb, the wine poured out a quarter-hin, the same measure as the oil); wine of the grapes, as the Targum, no other sort or substitute used for the sacred libation but the true fruit of the vine (Gill); the principle — the quantity of flour, oil, and wine augmented in proportion to the size of the animal, least with a lamb, more with a ram, most with a bullock, the accessory offering growing with the principal sacrifice (Clarke); the drink offering owning God as the giver of the gladness of life, figuring the pouring out of the soul in devotion and the blood of Christ poured out, as Paul speaks of being poured out as a drink offering (Phil 2:17).

themes: the fourth part of an hin of wine
wine of the grapes poured out

schools.summary: The larger meat offering for a ram — 'for a ram... two tenth deals of flour mingled with the third part of an hin of oil' (with a ram, the meat offering doubled in flour, two tenths instead of one, and increased in oil, a third-hin instead of a fourth, with a like increase of wine); the measure greater because the ram was a better and larger sacrifice than the lamb, and for the still greater bullock greater yet (three tenth deals) — 'the accessory sacrifice grows proportionably with the principal' (Wesley); God appointing the accompanying offering to match the dignity and size of the victim, the meat and drink offering always proportioned to the sacrifice, all measured by rule; the gradation lamb, ram, bullock running in due proportion through flour, oil, and wine; God's worship ordered with exactness and fitness.

themes: the accessory grows with the principal

schools.summary: The drink offering for a ram — 'the third part of an hin of wine for a drink offering, for a sweet savour unto the LORD' (the wine the same quantity as the oil of the meat offering, a third-hin for the ram as a fourth-hin for the lamb, the wine keeping pace with the oil and both with the size of the sacrifice, Gill); 'for a sweet savour unto the LORD' — the whole offering, flesh, flour, oil, and wine, ascending as a fragrance well-pleasing to God, the appointed order kept making the offering a sweet savour; the drink offering, like the meat offering, growing in proportion to the principal sacrifice, the ram's exceeding the lamb's and falling short of the bullock's; the poured-out wine owning God as the giver of gladness, figuring devotion poured out and the blood of Christ shed.

themes: the third part of an hin of wine
for a sweet savour unto the LORD
the libation matched to the sacrifice

schools.summary: The bullock and its occasions — 'a bullock for a burnt offering, or for a sacrifice in performing a vow, or peace offerings' (the bullock offered wholly as a burnt offering, or in fulfilment of a vow — 'a special vow' such as the Nazarite's, Num 6:2, Ellicott — or as a peace offering); peace offerings offered freely or by command, called peace or thank offerings by eminence because brought purely by way of gratitude to God with single respect to his goodness (Wesley); the bullock, being the largest offering, requiring the largest meat and drink offering, the accessory growing with the principal, the greatest sacrifice having the fullest accompaniment (Gill); the costliest victim honoured with the most abundant offering of flour, oil, and wine; the typology — the bullock wholly offered pointing to Christ, the great sacrifice.

themes: a bullock for a burnt offering
the greatest victim, the fullest offering

schools.summary: The great meat offering for a bullock — 'three tenth deals of flour mingled with half an hin of oil' (with a bullock, three tenths of an ephah of flour and half a hin of oil, and half a hin of wine, the largest of the three measures as the bullock was the largest of the three victims); much larger than for a lamb (one tenth) or ram (two tenths), the scale rising by even steps — one, two, three tenths of flour, a fourth, a third, a half of a hin of oil and wine — in fit proportion (Gill, Keil); God ordaining the accompanying offering to grow with the principal, the worship of God framed with exact and beautiful proportion; the lesson — our gifts to God answering to the greatness of his mercies; the typology — the abundant flour, oil, and wine pointing to the fulness of grace in Christ.

themes: the fullest offering crowns the scale

schools.summary: The great drink offering for a bullock — 'half an hin of wine, for an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD' (the wine the same quantity as the oil of the meat offering, half a hin for the bullock, the largest of the three measures, the wine keeping pace with the oil and both with the sacrifice, Gill); 'of a sweet savour unto the LORD' — the whole, flesh, flour, oil, and wine, ascending as a fragrance well-pleasing to God, the fullest offering brought in God's appointed order a sweet savour; the gradation complete — lamb, ram, bullock, each with its proportioned meat and drink offering, the wine rising from a fourth to a third to a half of a hin; the poured-out wine owning God as the giver of gladness, figuring the blood of Christ shed in fullest measure.

themes: of a sweet savour unto the LORD

schools.summary: The rule summed for every victim — 'thus shall it be done for one bullock, or for one ram, or for a lamb, or a kid' (the measures already given observed for each sort of animal sacrifice, the bullock with three tenths, the ram with two, the lamb or kid with one, each with its proportioned oil and wine); no difference made on account of the age of the beast, whether young or old, the rule going by the kind of victim not its years (Gill); God leaving nothing to the offerer's guess but fixing for every sacrifice its exact meat and drink offering, that all be done decently and in order, the worshipper knowing always what to bring; the kid named with the lamb, the lesser cattle sharing the smallest measure; the law providing for every case.

themes: for bullock, ram, lamb, or kid

schools.summary: The offerings reckoned by the number of victims — 'according to the number that ye shall prepare, so shall ye do to every one according to their number' (the meat and drink offerings multiplied according to the number of the animals sacrificed, as many beasts as offered, so many meal and drink offerings, each victim having its own full accompaniment of flour, oil, and wine, Gill/Wesley); the rule not only for a single sacrifice but for many, at the great feasts each of many bullocks, rams, and lambs attended by its proper meat and drink offering, reckoned by its kind and counted by the number; God having his worship furnished in full, no victim offered without its appointed gift however great the multitude; the abundance of God's mercies calling for abundance in devotion.

themes: so shall ye do to every one
the rule scales to the occasion

schools.summary: The rule binding on the native Israelite — 'all that are born of the country shall do these things after this manner, in offering an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD' (the indigenous Israelites, the home-born, as distinguished from the stranger of v14, Ellicott; all Israelites in offering their sacrifices to bring the meat and drink offerings in the quantity directed, Gill); the rule a binding statute for the whole people, none of the native-born exempt, whoever offered a sacrifice to offer it in this appointed manner with its proper accompaniment; 'of a sweet savour unto the LORD,' the offering so brought acceptable and well-pleasing; a uniform manner of worship for all Israel, the service of God one and the same, none offering after his own fashion but all after God's; God's worship admitting no private innovation.

themes: all that are born of the country
shall do these things after this manner

schools.summary: The stranger's offering like the native's — 'if a stranger sojourn with you... as ye do, so he shall do' (the foreigner dwelling among Israel who would offer a sacrifice to bring it in the very same manner, with the same meat and drink offerings, as the native-born, no lesser rule for him); the careful definition — not every foreigner but the proselyte of righteousness, one sprung from the heathen nations who had turned from idols to the true God and been received by circumcision into the covenant people, the uncircumcised stranger remaining in heathenism excluded (Gill, Calvin); the kindred laws of the stranger's welcome (Lev 19:33, Clarke); the convert admitted to the same worship and acceptance as the native, a presage of the future calling of the Gentiles into the one Church (Eph 2:13); God's house open to all who came in his appointed way.

themes: if a stranger sojourn with you
as ye do, so he shall do
the calling of the Gentiles foreshadowed

schools.summary: One ordinance for native and stranger — 'one ordinance shall be both for you of the congregation, and also for the stranger... as ye are, so shall the stranger be before the LORD' (not one rule for the home-born and another for the convert, but one and the same ordinance for both, the stranger who had joined the covenant people worshipping on the same terms and accepted on the same footing); 'before the LORD' — as to worship, the proselyte's sacrifices offered in the same manner and accepted on the same terms as the native's, a presage of the future calling of the Gentiles, when the partition wall should be broken down and Jew and Gentile made one in Christ (Gal 3:28, Wesley); even under the law the door open to the stranger who turned to God, made equal in worship; God no respecter of persons or nations.

themes: one ordinance for you and the stranger
as ye are, so shall the stranger be
a presage of the Gentiles' calling

schools.summary: One law and one manner for all — 'one law and one manner shall be for you, and for the stranger that sojourneth with you'; the double term distinguished — one and the same precept respecting these sacrifices, and one and the same way of offering them, both rule and rite identical for the home-born and the convert, and one and the same punishment for the breach in either (Gill); the law saying three times over (vv14-16) that the stranger who joined Israel should worship exactly as the native, driving it home that none think the convert of lesser standing or bound by a lighter rule; perfect equality in worship and acceptance between Israelite and proselyte; the gospel foreshadowed — one law and one manner for Jew and Gentile prefiguring the one body, one faith, one baptism (Eph 4:5).

themes: for you and for the stranger

schools.summary: The new word introducing the cake of the first dough — 'the LORD spake unto Moses' (God continuing to speak, the following law given at the same time as the laws of the meat and drink offerings, the new preface marking a distinct precept yet of the same occasion and gracious purpose, Gill); the law, like the foregoing, looking forward to the land, concerning the bread made from the corn of Canaan, belonging to the time of their settled possession not the wilderness; the fitness of its placing — having ordained the offerings of flour, oil, and wine, God now adds the offering of the first of the dough, that the very bread of the people's table own him as its giver; God giving his statutes one by one, each with its own word, an ordered series; God claiming the first of all his gifts.

themes: the law of the first dough
God claims the first of his gifts

schools.summary: The law bound to the land — 'when ye come into the land whither I bring you' (the offering of the cake belonging to the settled life of Canaan, when Israel should sow, reap, grind, and bake the corn of their own fields, not to be kept in the wilderness where they ate the manna, Gill); the renewed assurance 'whither I bring you,' God speaking of the land as certainly to be possessed, the promise standing firm to the rising generation despite the fathers' rebellion; the law binding the children of Israel only, the covenant people, to offer the cake, the dough of a Gentile free from it, the law of firstfruits belonging to those who held the land as God's gift; the people who would eat the bread of Canaan first owning him as the giver; God's claim following his gift.

themes: when ye come into the land
the law bound to the land

schools.summary: The offering before eating the bread of the land — 'when ye eat of the bread of the land, ye shall offer up an heave offering unto the LORD'; 'when ye eat' meaning 'when ye are about to eat,' before they actually partook, when the corn of Canaan had been ground into flour and mixed into dough, the first of it offered before any was eaten (Gill, Wesley); a 'heave offering,' lifted up and set apart for God and his priests, a token that the whole was his and from him; God's portion coming first, before the people took their own use, to eat first being to forget the giver; 'the bread' meaning the bread-corn, the produce of the harvest (Wesley); all enjoyment of God's gifts beginning with the acknowledgment of him, the firstfruits hallowing the whole, the believer eating his bread with thanksgiving.

themes: when ye eat of the bread of the land
an heave offering unto the LORD

schools.summary: The cake of the first dough — 'a cake of the first of your dough for an heave offering' (of the first dough, made from the first corn threshed, winnowed, and ground, a cake made and offered as a heave offering, the offering of the bread itself not merely the grain, Gill); the word arisoth, 'dough' or 'mixed meal,' a rare term found elsewhere only in Neh 10:37 and Ezekiel of this offering (Ellicott); why the offering was of the dough and not only the ears — another kind of firstfruits, the acknowledgment of God more lively in the bread itself, the food of daily life, than in raw grain (Calvin); 'if the firstfruit be holy, the lump is also holy' (Rom 11:16), the first cake sanctifying the whole baking and the whole people; the typology — the firstfruits pointing to Christ, the firstfruits of his people.

themes: a cake of the first of your dough
the first of the bread to God

schools.summary: The perpetual offering of the first dough — 'of the first of your dough ye shall give unto the LORD an heave offering in your generations' (the offering of the first cake not for one time only but rendered throughout their generations, as often as they baked the bread of the land); the reasons — an acknowledgment that the LORD was the sovereign Lord and possessor of heaven and earth and the true owner of the land of Canaan, and a thankful confession that their daily bread came from his hand (Gill); the fitness of so homely and constant an offering, as bread was the staple of daily life, the offering of its first a continual confession of dependence woven into the household round; the gift going as a heave offering to the priests (Neh 10:37); God owned and thanked not only at the feasts but in the daily bread.

themes: of the first of your dough
an heave offering in your generations
the first and best is God's

schools.summary: The case of sins of ignorance — 'if ye have erred, and not observed all these commandments' (rather 'if ye shall err and not observe,' Ellicott; every sin in truth an error, a missing of the mark, a wandering from God's commandments, Gill); the Jewish writers and Henry taking it chiefly of false worship or idolatry fallen into through the error of their teachers, a national failing; Wesley taking the commandments as those concerning the outward service and ceremonies, wherein this law differs from Lev 4:13; Moses enumerating only two of the four classes of sin offering of Leviticus — the congregation's and the individual's (Calvin); the gracious assumption that God's people will sometimes err in ignorance not defiant rebellion, and that he has made atonement even for such; sins of weakness not cutting off from God but met by the appointed sacrifice.

The whole body of the law in view — 'all that the LORD hath commanded you by the hand of Moses... and henceforward among your generations' (the whole compass of the laws given to Israel through Moses, whether moral, ceremonial, or judicial, the provision for sins of ignorance reaching to error in any of them, Gill); 'henceforward' rather 'and onward,' or thenceforward, nothing in the word determining whether later legislation is included, the law looking from the day of its giving forward through all the generations (Ellicott); God's mercy in providing atonement for ignorance not limited to a few commandments but covering the whole law, whatever the people transgressed unwittingly met by a sacrifice; the law acknowledging the people's frailty and making constant provision, the same Moses delivering the commands and the remedy for their unwitting breach.

themes: all that the LORD hath commanded

schools.summary: The offering for the congregation's error — 'if ought be committed by ignorance without the knowledge of the congregation... a young bullock for a burnt offering... and one kid of the goats for a sin offering' (a transgression fallen into by the people as a body, unwittingly, ignorance of the law's precepts, Gill); the contrast in the Hebrew shegagah — a transgression committed unwittingly, by error and inadvertence, as opposed to the presumptuous high-handed sin of v30, the passage turning on this distinction between the sin of weakness and the sin of defiance (Ellicott); a bullock offered whole in consecration and a kid as a sin offering for atonement, with their meat and drink offerings; this case the whole congregation, where Leviticus dealt also with the individual (Lev 4:2, Clarke); the typology — the sin offering pointing to Christ, the propitiation for the whole body of his people.

themes: without the knowledge of the congregation

schools.summary: The atonement and forgiveness of the congregation — 'the priest shall make an atonement for all the congregation... and it shall be forgiven them; for it is ignorance'; the priest making atonement on behalf of the whole people by the sin offering, a type of Christ, the propitiation not only for Israel but for his people throughout the world (1 Jn 2:2, Gill); 'it shall be forgiven them' — upon the atonement made, the guilt of their unwitting sin remitted, the sacrifice availing, God putting away the offence; 'for it is ignorance,' rather 'a sin of ignorance, or an error' (Ellicott), because not of defiance but inadvertence, capable of atonement; even sins through ignorance requiring an atonement — ignorance not making them no sins — yet God in mercy providing it (Clarke); the unwitting sinner neither excused without sacrifice nor left without remedy.

themes: the priest shall make an atonement

schools.summary: The forgiveness extended to the stranger — 'it shall be forgiven all the congregation... and the stranger that sojourneth among them; seeing all the people were in ignorance'; the grant of pardon said again for the certainty of it, that the people be assured the atonement availed, God doubling his word of mercy that faith may rest on it (Gill); the proselyte who dwelt with Israel sharing in the atonement and forgiveness as in the worship and the law, the mercy one for native and convert alike; 'seeing all the people were in ignorance' rather 'for in regard to all the people, it was done unwittingly' (Ellicott), because the whole body sinned not in defiance but ignorance, the atonement reaching all; the same forgiveness covering the stranger who joined himself to God's people, foreshadowing the one atonement of Christ for all nations.

themes: all the people were in ignorance

schools.summary: The offering for the individual's error — 'if any soul sin through ignorance, then he shall bring a she goat of the first year for a sin offering' (any private person who breaks a command, not rightly understanding it or not adverting to the circumstances of obeying, Gill); here, unlike Lev 4:27, no restriction to sins of commission, the provision covering sins of omission as well, the failure to do what was commanded as much as the doing of what was forbidden, when through ignorance (Ellicott); a she goat of the first year, a smaller and humbler victim than the congregation's bullock and kid, fitting the single offerer, yet a true sin offering availing for atonement; one law for native and stranger in these sins of ignorance; God's provision not only for great public sins but for the private unwitting failings of the single soul; the typology — Christ atoning for the secret faults of each.

themes: if any soul sin through ignorance
a she goat for a sin offering

schools.summary: The atonement and forgiveness of the individual — 'the priest shall make an atonement for the soul that sinneth ignorantly... and it shall be forgiven him'; the priest making atonement by offering the appointed sin offering for him, the same priestly mediation that availed for the whole people availing for the individual (Gill); 'when he sinneth by ignorance before the LORD' — the sin, though hidden from the man himself in his ignorance, before the LORD who saw it, yet being of ignorance not defiance covered by the atonement (Keil and Delitzsch); God dealing with the single soul as carefully as with the congregation, the humblest individual's unwitting sin met by the same mercy as the nation's; the typology — the priest's atonement pointing to Christ, the great High Priest, who atones for the secret and unwitting sins of each believer.

themes: atonement for the soul that sinneth ignorantly

schools.summary: One law of atonement for native and stranger — 'ye shall have one law for him that sinneth through ignorance, both for him that is born among the children of Israel, and for the stranger that sojourneth among them' (one and the same law, enjoining the she-goat for a sin offering, for both the home-born and the proselyte who sinned unwittingly, no different rule of atonement for the convert, Gill); as there was one ordinance for native and stranger in the offerings (vv15-16), so one law of forgiveness in their sins of ignorance, the convert equal not only in worship but in mercy; the stranger who joined himself to the LORD met, when he sinned in weakness, by the same atonement and pardon as the native, God's mercy knowing no national bound within his people; foreshadowing the one sacrifice of Christ availing alike for Jew and Gentile (Rom 3:30).

themes: one law for him that sinneth through ignorance
the unity of the people of God

schools.summary: The presumptuous sin and its doom — 'the soul that doeth ought presumptuously... the same reproacheth the LORD; and that soul shall be cut off'; 'presumptuously' literally 'with a high hand,' through pride, the Targum 'with an uncovered head,' openly and insolently, such avowedly confronting God's authority and setting their own will against his, sinning not in weakness or ignorance but in deliberate daring defiance (Ellicott, Gill, Henry); 'reproacheth the LORD' rather 'blasphemeth' (2 Kgs 19:22), setting God at defiance as if he were unable to punish (Wesley); for such sin no atonement appointed, bold transgression against the fullest light admitting no sacrifice, the sinner cut off (Clarke); the New Testament echo, 'if we sin wilfully... there remaineth no more sacrifice for sins' (Heb 10:26).

debates.question: What distinguishes the presumptuous sin from the sin of ignorance, and why is there no atonement for it?

sides.position: the difference lies not in the outward act but in the heart — the sin of ignorance a stumbling of weakness, capable of atonement; the presumptuous sin a deliberate, defiant 'high hand' against God, a reproach or blasphemy that despises his authority, and so admits of no sacrifice but cutting off (cf. Heb 10:26)

themes: cut off — no atonement for defiance

schools.summary: The ground of the presumptuous sinner's doom — 'because he hath despised the word of the LORD, and hath broken his commandment, that soul shall utterly be cut off; his iniquity shall be upon him'; such a one breaking the commandment not in ignorance or weakness but through contempt of it, despising it as a command of God and paying no regard to it, the very thing distinguishing him from the sinner of ignorance who reveres the command even while unwittingly breaking it (Gill); the doubled 'utterly be cut off' marking the certainty and severity of the excision; 'his iniquity shall be upon him' — the guilt resting on himself alone, not covered by any atonement nor borne away by any sacrifice; what shut him out of mercy not the greatness of the act but the contempt of the heart; the gospel parallel (Heb 10:29).

themes: despised the word of the LORD

schools.summary: The Sabbath-breaker as an instance of presumptuous sin — 'they found a man that gathered sticks upon the sabbath day'; the incident placed here to illustrate the foregoing law of the high-handed sinner, the man despising the word of the LORD and breaking his commandment (v31) in open defiance (Clarke, Ellicott); the law of the Sabbath plain and positive, well known, so the gathering of sticks no sin of ignorance but a known and wilful transgression in the face of a clear command (Ex 31:14, Wesley); the Jewish opinion that the incident, though recorded here, may have happened earlier in the first year, Calvin judging it best to follow the order of Moses' narrative; the broken Sabbath, the sign of the covenant and weekly confession of God as Creator and Redeemer, a fit instance of the presumptuous sin.

The offender brought to judgment — 'they that found him gathering sticks brought him unto Moses and Aaron, and unto all the congregation'; those who discovered him not letting it pass but bringing him before the appointed authorities, the Targum and Jarchi's tradition that they first admonished him to desist but he would not, persisting in his defiance, deepening the presumption (Gill); 'all the congregation' meaning the rulers of the congregation, the elders who judged, before whom with Moses and Aaron the case was laid (Wesley); the offence not punished by private hands or in haste but brought before the lawful authority, that judgment be done in due order and by warrant; the bringing of him in showing the people's sense of the gravity of an open profanation of the Sabbath, the sign of the covenant.

themes: brought him unto Moses and Aaron
order kept in dealing with sin

schools.summary: The offender held until God should direct — 'they put him in ward, because it was not declared what should be done to him'; placed in a prison in the camp, perhaps the same in which the blasphemer had been kept (Lev 24:12), the 'they' being Moses, Aaron, and the seventy rulers (Gill, Wesley); not the death penalty itself in doubt, for the law had already declared the Sabbath-breaker should be put to death (Ex 31:14), but the manner of his death — by what kind of execution he should be cut off — not yet determined, which God now appointed (Wesley); the leaders not presuming to invent a punishment nor to act before they had God's word, but holding him in custody until the LORD should direct, doing nothing in so grave a matter without express warrant; the care of Moses and the rulers to follow God exactly.

themes: it was not declared what should be done
the manner of death not yet known

schools.summary: God's sentence on the Sabbath-breaker — 'the LORD said unto Moses, The man shall be surely put to death: all the congregation shall stone him with stones without the camp'; Moses consulting the LORD in the tabernacle, at the most holy place, from above the mercy seat where God promised to commune with him (Ex 25:22), the judgment coming not from Moses but from God (Gill); the death penalty already threatened in the law confirmed and ordered to be executed, the wilful profanation of the Sabbath a capital crime; the manner — stoned by the whole congregation, outside the camp, the public execution marking the public guilt and the casting of the offender from the holy community; not the gathering of sticks in itself but the open contempt of God's plain command drawing so heavy a doom; the typology — Christ suffering without the gate (Heb 13:12).

themes: the man shall be surely put to death
God's holiness not to be despised

schools.summary: The sentence executed in obedience — 'all the congregation brought him without the camp, and stoned him with stones, and he died; as the LORD commanded Moses'; the people doing precisely what God appointed, in the manner and place he named, the repeated 'as the LORD commanded' marking exact compliance; 'all the congregation' — what was done by order of Moses and the elders ascribed to the whole congregation, though the man was brought out and stoned by a few acting on behalf of all, the act the people's, done in their name and authority (Gill); the execution 'without the camp,' putting the offender from the community he defiled; the law of the high-handed sinner (vv30-31) shown in act, the despiser cut off, his iniquity upon him, no atonement intervening; the typology — Christ suffering without the gate (Heb 13:12).

themes: the law of presumptuous sin enacted

schools.summary: The new word introducing the fringes — 'the LORD spake unto Moses'; coming after the foregoing laws and the order for stoning the Sabbath-breaker, what follows aptly joined, designed to put the people in mind of God's commandments that they might not forget and transgress (Gill); provision having just been made in the sin offerings for the pardon of sins of ignorance, God now providing an expedient for the preventing of such sins — the fringes, a constant reminder, that the people not fall into the very errors for which atonement had been appointed (Henry); God first providing the remedy for sin (the atonement), then the means to keep from sin (the reminder), his care reaching to both the pardon of past failings and the prevention of future ones; the new word introducing not a burden but a help.

themes: God prevents as well as pardons

schools.summary: The fringes and the blue ribband — 'fringes in the borders of their garments... a ribband of blue'; tassels or threads standing out on the corners of the outer garment, on the four borders (Deut 22:12), each marked with a cord of blue (Wesley, Ellicott); all Israelites — priests, Levites, common Israelites, proselytes, freed servants — bound to wear them, but not the Gentiles, the token belonging to the covenant people (Gill); the fringes emblematical of the commandments of God, no natural likeness between a tassel and a precept yet, divinely appointed for this use, serving as a constant sign to call the commandments to mind (Clarke); the blue the colour of the heavens, marking the commandments from above and drawing the thoughts upward; the abuse the Pharisees enlarging their borders for show (Mt 23:5), the fringe meant for remembrance not display.

themes: fringes in the borders of their garments

schools.summary: The purpose of the fringes — 'that ye may look upon it, and remember all the commandments of the LORD, and do them... and that ye seek not after your own heart and your own eyes'; the fringe a thing to be seen, the sight calling to mind the commandments, the remembrance leading to the doing, the eye teaching the heart and the heart moving the hand; God's only object to exercise the Jews in constant meditation upon the law, that it never slip from their thoughts (Calvin); the warning 'that ye seek not after your own heart and your own eyes, after which ye use to go a whoring' (rather 'go not about after your own heart,' Ellicott), the fringe checking the people from following the wandering desires of their hearts and the lusts of their eyes, which led them into idolatry; the heart left to itself straying, needing the constant reminder of God's word; the blue thread making the fringe the more visible (Wesley).

themes: all the commandments of the LORD
seek not after your own heart and eyes

schools.summary: The end of the fringes in holiness — 'that ye may remember, and do all my commandments, and be holy unto your God'; the chain — the looking leading to remembering, the remembering to doing, the doing to holiness, the fringe no end in itself but a means to the keeping of the commandments and the sanctifying of the people; the purpose repeated, that the end and use of the fringes be particularly noted, lest the people rest in the outward ceremony and neglect the obedience it was meant to serve, the sign nothing without the substance (Gill); 'be holy unto your God' — purged from sin and sincerely devoted to God (Wesley); holiness reached through obedience, obedience through remembrance, remembrance helped by the appointed token; the mere wearing without the remembering and doing profiting nothing.

themes: that ye may remember, and do

schools.summary: The redemption from Egypt as the ground of obedience — 'I am the LORD your God, which brought you out of the land of Egypt, to be your God: I am the LORD your God'; their Creator, Preserver, and Benefactor, their Lord and covenant God and Father, who had redeemed them from bondage (Gill); 'to be your God' — God confirming the command to be holy (v40) by this reason, that he redeemed them for this very purpose, that he might be their God and be honoured by them, the deliverance laying them under the obligation of obedience and holiness (Calvin); though justly displeased and keeping them forty years in the wilderness, God will not utterly cast them off but continues their God, the covenant holding (Wesley); grace the spring of duty, redemption the foundation of all the law; the doubled 'I am the LORD your God' pressing the truth home.

themes: I am the LORD your God
